● It involves three steps: ○ **Constitution of a dedicated commission** to conduct an empirical study on OBC backwardness in local bodies. ○ **Reservation based on recommendations** of the commission to ensure evidence-based quotas. ○ **Capping total reservations** (for SCs, STs, and OBCs) at not more than 50% of total seats.
● This move ensures that **OBC representation** in urban governance is **legally sound**, **data-driven**, and **constitutionally compliant**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Jharkhand has finished collecting data on the population of Other Backward Classes (OBCs) from all districts of the state, in order to determine quotas for OBCs in urban local bodies.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the BRAHMOS missile,
consider the following statements:
I.  	 It is a hypersonic ballistic missile.
II. 	 It is a two-stage missile with a liquid-fueled
ramjet engine.
III. 	 It follows the "Fire and Forget" principle and
has a low radar signature.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **BrahMos** is a **supersonic cruise missile** jointly developed by India and Russia, named after the Brahmaputra and Moskva rivers. It is capable of being launched from land, air, sea, and submarine platforms. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● It is a two-stage missile, with a solid propellant booster and **liquid-fueled ramjet engine**, reaching speeds of up to Mach 3. **So, statement II is correct.**
● It follows a **“fire-and-forget”** principle with high precision, low radar signature, and shorter engagement time. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The missile carries a **200–300 kg conventional warhead** and has a **range of up to 400 km**, with newer versions aiming for extended range and improved maneuverability.
● **BrahMos-NG (Next Generation) is a lighter, more versatile missile** with a range of 290 km and a speed of Mach 3.5, suitable for multi-platform deployment, including submarine torpedo tubes.
● BrahMos-II is under development as a hypersonic cruise missile with speeds up to Mach 7, using scramjet engine technology, and will be crucial for countering threats in the Indo-Pacific. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The development of a new, compact air-launched version of the BrahMos supersonic cruise missile, the BrahMos-NG (Next Generation).

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Region Country
I. - Alcatraz Mauritius
II. - Bushehr Iran
III. - Bagram Afghanistan
IV. - Starbase United States of America
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● **Alcatraz Island**, nicknamed “The Rock”, is a 22-acre island in San Francisco Bay, California, **USA**. **So, pair I is** **not correctly matched.**
○ It became the site of **California’s first lighthouse** **in 1854**. ○ Recently, the U.S. President has directed a plan to rebuild and expand Alcatraz, reviving interest in its historic legacy.
● **Bushehr** is a **port city** located on the **Gulf coast of Iran** and is home to Iran’s only operational nuclear power plant. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
○ The Bushehr Nuclear Power Plant is a symbol of Iran-Russia nuclear cooperation, with Rosatom (Russia's state atomic agency) playing a key role in its development. Recently, **Tehran and** **Moscow** inaugurated a **new construction phase** for the **second reactor** at the plant.
● **Bagram Air Base** is a **strategically significant military airfield** located in **Afghanistan**, about **50** **km north of Kabul**. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
○ It served as the **largest U.S. military base** **in** **Afghanistan** and played a crucial role during the War on Terror and the U.S.-led NATO operations.
● **Starbase** is a **private town in the United States**, located in **Texas**, near **Brownsville**, and was formerly known as **Boca** **Chica Village**. **So, pair IV  is correctly matched.**
○ It serves as the hub for **SpaceX’s launch and rocket** **manufacturing operations**, including testing of the **Starship rocket system**. **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: International Organizations (UN, IMF, World Bank, ASEAN, BRICS, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with reference to the Extended Fund Facility (EFF)?
I. - It assists countries facing medium-term Balance of Payments (BoP) problems.
II.	  It can be availed by all the member countries of the International Monetary Fund (IMF).
III. 	 It provides for extendable and flexible debt repayment facilities.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Extended Fund Facility (EFF)** is a **lending arrangement** of the  **International Monetary** **Fund (IMF)**, established in 1974 to **assist countries facing medium-term balance of payments** **(BoP) problems** due to structural economic weaknesses. It provides loans.  **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● It provides support for implementing medium-term structural reforms, with longer program durations and extended repayment terms. **All IMF member countries with actual or potential external** **financing needs are eligible** for this facility. **So, statement II is correct.**
● EFF **programs typically last 3 years, extendable to 4 years**, and repayment is made over 4½ to 10 years in 12 equal semi-annual installments. India borrowed $3.9 billion under the EFF in 1980, though it did not draw the full amount as its economy recovered. During the 1991 economic crisis, India borrowed $2.2 billion under two standby arrangements. By the 2000s, with an improved economy and strong BoP position, India became a creditor to the IMF. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The International Monetary Fund (IMF) cleared a $3 billion-Extended Fund Facility (EFF) for Sri Lanka.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Akashteer system of India:
I. - It integrates real-time surveillance autonomous drone swarms, and instantaneous AI-powered response networks.
II.	 It has been developed by the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Akashteer System** represents a major leap in India’s air defence network, designed to provide seamless command and control over multi-layered air defence assets. Developed with modern warfare in mind, Akashteer System integrates cutting-edge technology, automation, and real-time data processing to counter diverse aerial threats efficiently.
● **Akashteer** is an advanced, AI-enabled, multi-layered defence coordination system leveraging indigenous technologies. It **integrates real-time surveillance from ISRO’s Earth observation satellites**, NAVIC-based precision targeting, autonomous drone swarms, and instantaneous AI-powered response networks. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Developed with **Bharat Electronics Limited’s (BEL) battlefield processors** and mobile command units, Akashteer System forms a dynamic combat web, capable of detecting, analysing, and neutralising aerial threats autonomously, without the need for direct human intervention. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Science & Technology|Defence Technology
**Relevance:** At the media briefing on Operation Sindoor, military officers displayed a picture of the Integrated Air Command and Control System (IACCS) node of the Indian Air Force (IAF).

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ramsar Sites, World Heritage Sites, UNESCO Heritage Sites, WHS, Biodiversity Heritage Sites, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to Wular Lake, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a freshwater lake fed by the Chenab River.
II. 	 It is recognised as a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Wular Lake**, located in the Bandipore district of **Jammu and Kashmir**, is the **largest freshwater**
**lake in India** and the second largest in Asia.  It lies at an altitude of 1,580 m at the foothills of the
Haramuk Mountain and is fed by the **Jhelum River**. **So, statement I is**
**not correct.**
● It was formed due to **tectonic activity** and is believed to be a remnant
of the ancient Satisar Lake. At its centre lies the Zaina Lank, a small island
built by King Zainul-Abi-Din.
● In **1990**, Wular Lake was designated a **Ramsar Wetland of**
**International Importance**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● It serves as a critical habitat for **wintering, staging, and breeding**
**birds**, including species like the black-eared kite, Eurasian sparrow hawk, short-toed eagle, and
Himalayan monal. The lake also supports rich fish biodiversity, contributing to **60% of Jammu and**
**Kashmir’s total fish production**.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Wular Lake is slowly choking up due to the ingress of silt-laden waters gushing in the form of
streams from mountains in its catchment.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: In the context of Jainism, the practice of ‘Sallekhana’ refers to:

options.a: Strict observance of meditation to attain the state of enlightenment
options.b: Repenting for the sins that one might have done and vow to never repeat them
options.c: Voluntarily fasting to death by gradually reducing the intake of food and water
options.d: Renunciation of property to control one's desires

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **'Sallekhana'** is the Jain religious practice of **voluntary fasting unto death.** 'Santhara', or Sallekhana, is a centuries-old Jain tradition. It involves **gradually reducing the intake of food and liquids**. It is not considered as a suicide by Jain scholars because it is not an act of passion, nor does it deploy poisons or weapons. It can be performed by both Jain ascetics and householders. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Pratikraman** is a process during which Jains **repent for their sins during their** **daily life**, and remind themselves not to repeat them. The types of Pratikraman include Devasi, Rai, Pakhi, Chaumasi and Samvatsari.
● **Pratyakhyan is a formal renunciation of certain activities,** which reduces or stops the inflow of the karmas. It helps us to learn to control one's desires and prepares for the bigger renunciation.
**Relevance:** Recently, Sallekhana was recorded in the Golden Book of World Records.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
The Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project (KLIP),
which is considered as the largest lift irrigation project in India, is being built on which one of the following rivers?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project (KLIP) is on** **the Godavari river** in Telangana. It will be the world’s largest multi-stage lift irrigation project. The project spanning Telangana’s Jayashankar Bhupalpally district across the **border** **from Maharashtra** will supply water for irrigation, industrial, and domestic uses over a swath of northern Telangana.
○ In lift irrigation projects, water does not rely on gravity to flow in canals from higher ground to lower. Instead, **water is lifted by means of pumps or surge pools** **to a main delivery chamber** at the highest point in the project, from where it is distributed to the beneficiary fields. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A report by National Dam Safety Authority (NDSA) has pointed out serious concerns on Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Who amongst the following are the members of the committee to select the Director of the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)?
I. - The Prime Minister of India
II. 	 The Union Home Minister
III. 	 The Leader of Opposition in the Lok Sabha
IV. 	 The Chief Justice of India
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)** is the premier investigating police agency in India. It functions under the superintendence of the Department of Personnel, under the Ministry of Personnel, Pension & Public Grievances.
● The Director of the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) is recommended by a committee consisting of:
○ the **Prime Minister of India. So, point I is correct.** ○ **the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha. So, point III is correct.** ○ **the Chief Justice of India** (or a Judge of the Supreme Court nominated by the CJI). **So, point** **IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What is the maximum tenure of the CBI Director?**
● Director of CBI has been provided **security of two year tenure,** by the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003.
● In November 2021, the government brought in an ordinance to amend the Central
Vigilance Commission (CVC) Act, 2003 as well as the Delhi Special Police Establishment
(DSPE) Act, 1946 to empower it to give three one-year extensions to ED chiefs and
the CBI directors if it so desires. The ordinance was subsequently passed by Parliament
a month later.
○ The two year period for which the initial appointment has been made could, in
**public interest**, be extended up to one year at a time. This can be done **only**
**on the recommendation of the Committee** which is constituted for their
appointments. Further, no such extension shall be granted after the completion
of a period of five years in total including the period mentioned in the initial
appointment. Thus, **a CBI Director can have a maximum tenure of five**
**years.**
**Relevance:** The Government has extended the tenure of CBI Director Praveen Sood for one year

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: International Organizations (UN, IMF, World Bank, ASEAN, BRICS, etc.)
questions.questionText: The terms Jurisdictions under Increased Monitoring” and “High-Risk Jurisdictions subject to a Call for Action” are associated with:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Financial Action Task Force (FATF)** which was established in 1989 by the G7 to **examine** **and develop measures to combat money laundering**. It **originally included the G7 countries**, the European Commission and eight other countries.
● The FATF was given responsibility to examine money laundering techniques and trends, review the action already taken at a national or international level, and to set out measures needed to combat money laundering.  In 2001, the FATF expanded its mandate to also combat terrorist financing. Since 2019, the FATF has had an open-ended mandate, after originally operating under fixed-terms.
● **The FATF identified jurisdictions with weak measures to combat money laundering and** **terrorist financing** (Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing regimes) in two public documents that are issued three times a year: in February, June and October.
● The **grey list**, officially **“Jurisdictions under Increased Monitoring”**, comprises countries that have significant deficiencies in their AML/CFT regimes but are **actively working with the FATF to** **address these “within agreed timeframes”**. As the name suggests, countries in the grey list are subject to increased monitoring by the FATF. Currently, there are 24 countries in this list.
● The **black list**, officially **“High-Risk Jurisdictions subject to a Call for Action”**, comprises countries with **“serious strategic deficiencies”** in their AML/CFT regimes. The FATF urges all other jurisdictions to not only apply enhanced due diligence while dealing with countries in this list.
● Three countries are in the black list at the moment: **North Korea, Myanmar, and Iran**. They have been slapped with mandated economic sanctions by FATF member countries as a result.
● The FATF has a set of recommendations to “ensure a **coordinated global response to prevent** **organised crime, corruption and terrorism**”. These help countries to follow best practices to thwart money laundering and terror financing, and go after criminals engaged in these illegal practices. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India is expected to approach the Financial Action Task Force to add Pakistan to its grey list during its next meeting.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: In the context of defence technology of India, what is ‘Nagastra-1’?

options.a: A multi-role, multi-mission new generation fighter aircraft
options.b: An indigenously developed loitering munition system
options.c: A Beyond Visual Range (BVR) supersonic cruise missile
options.d: A laser based directed-energy weapon system

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Nagastra-1:** It is India’s **first indigenous Loitering Munition**. It is a UAV-based system. It has the ability to hover over the target, and that is why it is known as loitering munition. It **strikes in kamikaze** **mode, wiping off its target as well as itself.**
● Developed indigenously, it boasts the capability to **neutralize hostile threats with GPS-enabled** **precision strikes**, exhibiting an accuracy of 2 meters.  This man-portable fixed-wing electric UAV offers an endurance of 30 minutes, a man-in-loop range of 15 kilometres, and an autonomous mode range of 30 kilometres.
● Equipped with day and night surveillance cameras and a 1-kilogram High Explosive Fragmenting Warhead, the Loiter Munition stands ready to combat soft-skin targets effectively. Its unique features like **abort,** **recover and reuse**, facilitated by a parachute recovery mechanism, set it apart from similar systems globally.
● Nagastra-1 is a man-portable system with an all-up weight of 30 kg split across two rucksacks, including a ground control station, communication control, payload and pneumatic launcher. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Indian army inducted indigenous Nagastra-1 Precision "Kamikaze Drones".

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: INSV Kaundinya, the Indian Navy’s recently inducted stitched sail ship, is inspired by a 5thcentury CE depiction of a vessel found in which of the following historical sites?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Indian Navy has formally inducted the ancientstyle stitched sail ship, naming it **INSV** **Kaundinya**, a vessel modelled on a 5th-century CE depiction of a ship in the **Ajanta Caves**.
○ The inspiration for the vessel comes from Cave 2 at Ajanta in Chhatrapati Sambhaji Nagar (formerly Aurangabad), part of the **UNESCO World Heritage Site**. ○ Among the 30 rock-cut caves, six preserve murals from the early centuries of the Common Era. The mural in Cave 2 features a large merchant vessel with a high stem and stern, three oblong sails attached to masts, and steering oars. ○ This is one of the earliest surviving paintings that narrates the tale of India’s international sea trade. ○ The Ajanta murals largely illustrate **Jataka Katha** **(stories from the Buddha’s past lives)** **and** **Avadana Katha** **(narratives of heroic virtue)**. The ship painting is a part of the Purna Avadana, a mythical Buddhist narrative. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Who was the legendary mariner Kaundinya?**
● Long before colonial ships reached **Southeast Asia**, an Indian mariner named
Kaundinya **undertook a sea voyage that would alter the course of regional**
**history**. In a time when crossing the seas was seen as sacrilegious by many back home,
Kaundinya sailed fearlessly beyond the Indian subcontinent to the Mekong Delta.
● According to both historical sources and legend, Kaundinya was a Brahmin, most likely
from South India, who **ventured across the Bay of Bengal** sometime between the
1st and 2nd century CE.
● The influence of Kaundinya’s Indian heritage was profound. Under his rule and through
successive generations, Hinduism, Sanskrit, Indian art, administrative systems, and
temple architecture began to take root in the region.
**Relevance:**  The Indian Navy recently inducted and named an ‘ancient stitched sail ship’ as the INSV Kaundinya at a ceremonial event held at the Naval Base in Karwar.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText: Which one of the following species are known to exhibit ‘lek-breeding’, where males perform elaborate mating displays at specific locations to attract females?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Kakapo is one of the largest of the parrots**, so large that it cannot fly. It is a nocturnal, flightless parrot. It is native to New Zealand and its numbers have been dropping since the arrival of Europeans.
● It is nocturnal, herbivorous, and the only lek-breeding parrot species. Male Kakapo put on displays at fixed locations to attract female attention, and they don’t help to raise any offspring. It’s called lek breeding.
○ Lekking is a spectacular mating system in which **males maintain tightly organized clustering** **of territories during the mating season, and females visit these leks for mating**.
● Lek-breeding behaviour includes the digging of bowl-shaped depressions in the ground, which help amplify mating calls across long distances. Adult males and females meet only to breed, and the females raise their chicks alone. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Environment|Terrestrial and Aquatic Ecosystem
**Relevance:** A team of scientists has successfully used artificial insemination to breed wild endangered parrots in New Zealand.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the EPIC Number:
I. - It is a unique alphanumeric number assigned to every voter in India.
II. 	 It is assigned by the Chief Electoral Officer.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Elector Photo Identity Cards (EPIC):** The **Election Commission of India** provides every
registered voter in India with a unique identification number. One can find this number on your voter
card. This unique number is a Voter ID or EPIC number. The EPIC number in voter ID is a 10-digit alpha-
numeric code. **So, statement I is correct and statement II is not correct.**
● An EPIC number proves that you are a registered voter in India. The EPIC number  must be **present**
**on the electoral list or voter list to cast a vote** during the elections.
● The EPIC number signifies that **an individual’s voter identification information is digitally stored**
**in a colored photo identity format**. One can conveniently access this information through your
DigiLocker account or from the National Voters' Service Portal (NVSP).
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Election Commission (EC) recently declared that it has resolved the long-pending problem of
duplication of voter ID card numbers.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Irrigation Project Associated River
I. - Mekedatu Godavari
II. - Polavaram Krishna
III. - Kaleshwaram Cauvery
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Mekedatu dam** is a multi-purpose project involving the construction of a balancing reservoir near Kanakapura **across the Cauvery River** in the Ramanagara district of **Karnataka**. The project is proposed at the confluence of the **Cauvery River with its tributary Arkavathi.** The project is envisioned to provide a sustainable drinking water supply to Bengaluru, addressing the city’s growing demand due to rapid urbanisation. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Polavaram** is a major irrigation project on **Godavari River.** The project is considered as the ‘lifeline' of Andhra Pradesh. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.**
○ The dam is located near Polavaram village in the West Godavari district of Andhra Pradesh, an area bordering Chhattisgarh and Odisha. It aims to address water scarcity, boost agricultural productivity, and generate power.
● **The** **Kaleshwaram** **Lift** **Irrigation**
**Project (KLIP) is on the Godavari river**
in Telangana. It will be the world’s largest multi-stage lift irrigation
project. The project spanning Telangana’s Jayashankar Bhupalpally
district across the **border from Maharashtra** will supply water for
irrigation, industrial, and domestic uses over a swath of northern
Telangana. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ In lift irrigation projects, water does not rely on gravity to flow in canals from higher ground to
lower. Instead, **water is lifted by means of pumps or surge pools to a main delivery**
**chamber** at the highest point in the project, from where it is distributed to the beneficiary fields.
**So, none of the pairs given above is correctly matched.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A report by National Dam Safety Authority (NDSA) has pointed out serious concerns on
Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is **not** correct with reference to the Central Equipment Identity Register (CEIR) Portal?

options.a: It is an initiative of the Department of Telecommunications (DoT).
options.b: It uses the International Mobile Equipment Identity (IMEI) number to identify and track lost/stolen mobile devices.
options.c: It allows tracking of mobile devices even if the Subscriber Identity Module (SIM) card is changed.
options.d: Once a phone is blocked on CEIR, it can be used only for data services but not for voice calls.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Central Equipment Identity Register (CEIR)** is a centralised database of mobile equipment identifiers. **It is developed by the Department of Telecommunications (DoT)**, under the **Union** **Ministry of Communications.**
● The CEIR is implemented to **manage and track mobile devices using their International Mobile** **Equipment Identity (IMEI) numbers.** It will help in combating mobile device theft and misuse. The Portal can be used to block stolen and lost smartphones. Using the CEIR website, users can block a stolen or lost mobile, unblock a found mobile and they can even use the website to find more details about a second-hand smartphone.
○ It allows users to quickly lodge a complaint regarding a lost electronic gadget with an IMEI (International Mobile Equipment Identity) number**.** The IMEI uniquely identifies an individual mobile device. ○ The IMEI is unique to every Mobile Equipment and thereby provides a means for controlling access to GSM networks based on Model or individual units. The IMEI consists of a number of fields totaling 15 digits having digits from 0 to 9. The first eight digits are called Type Approval Code (TAC).
● The portal allows users to track the lost or stolen mobile phones through IMEI numbers **even if the** **SIM cards are changed or disabled.** When you block a smartphone on CEIR’s website, **it will be** **blocklisted on the central database and won’t be able to use it.** After the successful submission of a blocking request, the user's phone is blocked within 24 hours. After the phone has been blocked, **it** **cannot be used on any network across India.** A device blacklisted on one network will not work on other networks, even if the SIM card is changed. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** Telangana leads in recovery of stolen and lost phones through CEIR portal.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding SPICED scheme:
I. - It is an initiative of the Spices Board under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry.
II. It provides financial assistance to farmers for infrastructure development.
III. 	 It aims to enable stakeholders to comply with international food safety standards.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **SPICED Scheme** (Sustainability in Spice Sector through Progressive, Innovative and Collaborative Interventions for Export Development) is a flagship initiative launched by the **Spices Board under the** **Ministry of Commerce and Industry** for the financial year 2025–26. Its key objective is to enhance sustainability, improve productivity and strengthen India’s spice export competitiveness. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● One of the core components of the SPICED scheme is **post-harvest improvement**. It provides **financial support to individual spice farmers, SHGs, and Farmer Producer Organizations** to procure modern machines such as spice polishers, dryers, turmeric boilers, mint distillation units, and grading machines. These machines help reduce losses, improve the shelf life and quality of the spices, and ensure compliance with international export standards. **So, statement II is correct.**
● A major innovation of the SPICED scheme is the use of **geo-tagging technology**. All major activities and interventions under the scheme will be **geo-tagged** using digital tools to ensure **greater** **transparency, real-time monitoring**, and **effective fund utilization**.
● The scheme promotes **export development** and focuses on enabling stakeholders to **comply with** **international food safety and phytosanitary standards**. It aims to enhance the global competitiveness of Indian spices by ensuring they meet stringent import requirements in foreign markets, thus boosting export potential. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Spices Board launched the SPICED scheme for the financial year 2025–26.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The International Day for Biological Diversity is observed on 22 May every year.
**Statement II:** On 22 May, the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) was adopted at the Rio Earth Summit in 1992.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Day for Biological Diversity** is commemorated on **May 22** annually to promote understanding and awareness of biodiversity-related issues. The day serves as a global platform for highlighting the value of **ecosystems, species, and genetic diversity** and the urgent need to protect them amidst rising threats like climate change, habitat loss, pollution, and overexploitation. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD)** was formally adopted on **May 22, 1992**, during the **Rio Earth Summit**, a landmark international environmental conference. Initially, the International Day for Biodiversity was celebrated on December 29, the day the CBD came into force in 1993. However, in 2000, the UN General Assembly shifted the observance to **May 22 to reflect the actual** **adoption date of the Convention** and to give the day greater symbolic importance in aligning global efforts with the goals of the CBD. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** International Day for Biological Diversity 2025 was celebrated recently.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the International Booker Prize:
I.  	 It is awarded to a work that is translated recognizing both the author and translator.
II. 	 Recently, a Kannada language work has been awarded this prize for the first time.
III. ‘	Heart Lamp’ is the first short story collection to receive the prize.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? a) 	 I only
(b) 	 I, II and III
(c) 	 II only
(d) 	 II and III only
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Booker Prize** is one of the most prestigious literary awards in the world, recognizing excellence in **fiction translated into English** and published in the **UK or Ireland**. It is specifically designed to honor **translated works of fiction** from around the world. The award celebrates the literary merit of the book and also acknowledges the translator's crucial role in making the work accessible to an English-speaking audience. Both the **author and the translator** receive equal recognition and share the prize money equally. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Banu Mushtaq’s win in 2025 marks a **historic milestone**, as she is the **first Kannada-language** **author** to receive the **International Booker Prize**. Her victory brought regional Indian literature to the global stage and drew attention to the literary vibrancy of Kannada writing. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
○ Heart Lamp, authored by Banu Mushtaq, is not only the first Kannada work to win but also the **first short story collection ever** to win the **International Booker Prize**. This is significant because the award has historically favored novels. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Banu Mushtaq was deeply involved in the **Bandaya movement**, a Kannada literary and socio-political movement that emerged in the 1970s and aimed to challenge caste oppression, gender inequality and social injustice**.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Banu Mushtaq became the first Kannada-language author to receive the International Booker Prize.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: The Lunar Polar Exploration Mission (LUPEX), a collaborative lunar mission between ISRO (India) and JAXA (Japan), is intended to act as a successor to which one of the following earlier successful lunar missions?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Lunar Polar Exploration Mission (LUPEX)** is an upcoming and highly significant collaborative mission between **ISRO (India)** and **JAXA (Japan)** aimed at exploring the **lunar south pole**, particularly the **Permanently Shadowed Regions (PSRs)** that may contain **volatile elements** such as water ice.
● LUPEX (also referred to as Chandrayaan-5) marks the fifth installment in India’s Chandrayaan lunar program and is intended to **build upon the legacy of Chandrayaan-1**, 2, and 3. The mission is part of India's long-term lunar vision, including plans for a human Moon landing by 2040.
● LUPEX builds directly on the achievements of earlier missions on Chandrayaan, especially leveraging the **technical experience from Chandrayaan-3** and the **scientific knowledge** from Chandrayaan-1 and 2. It will use an Indian-made lander, a Japanese-made rover, and instruments from **ISRO, JAXA,** **ESA and NASA**, indicating a **continuation and expansion** of India's lunar exploration capabilities. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** ISRO and JAXA  jointly conducted the third face-to-face Technical Interface Meeting (TIM-3), towards the Chandrayaan-5/LUPEX mission.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to Phased Array Radar Systems,
consider the following statements:
I. - They operate on the principle of
beamforming where the direction of the radar
beam is controlled electronically.
II. 	 They are capable of tracking and detecting
multiple targets simultaneously.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Phased Array Radar (PAR)** system represents a sophisticated evolution in **radar technology,** designed to improve detection, tracking and response capabilities across defense, aerospace, and surveillance sectors. Unlike conventional radar systems that rely on mechanically rotating antennas to scan their environment, PARs **employ a grid of numerous small antenna elements controlled** **electronically.**
● **Beamforming** is the core operating principle of PARs. It involves electronically controlling the phase of signals emitted or received by each antenna element in the array. By adjusting these phases, constructive interference is created in a specific direction, effectively steering the beam without physical movement. This **electronic beam steering** enables the radar to quickly switch between targets or scan large areas within microseconds. It provides flexibility, faster target acquisition, and reduced mechanical wear, crucial for applications like missile defense, aircraft surveillance, and autonomous systems. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ A major advantage of phased array radars is their lack of moving parts for beam direction. Traditional radar systems use mechanically rotating dishes, which are slower and subject to wear and tear. In contrast, PARs steer their beams **electronically**, leading to **instantaneous** **changes in field of view**, which is highly beneficial for high-speed tracking and multiple-object monitoring.
● They are capable of **multi-target detection and tracking** due to their fast beam-switching capabilities and high data processing speeds. For example, **modern warships** equipped with PARs can simultaneously monitor over 100 targets, including aircraft, ships, and incoming missiles. This multitasking capability makes PARs extremely useful in defense systems, especially in scenarios where multiple threats must be identified and tracked in real time, such as in naval warfare, air defense, and space surveillance. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In the recent India-Pakistan conflict, India's air defense systems, including the Akash SAM with its Rajendra phased-array radar, played a crucial role in intercepting Pakistani UAVs.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding
Shingles disease:
I. - It is a bacterial disease.
II. 	 It is caused by the same agent that is
responsible for chickenpox.
III. 	 It is listed as Neglected Tropical Disease by
the World Health Organization (WHO).
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Shingles,** also known as herpes zoster, is a **viral infection** caused by the reactivation of the **Varicella-** **Zoster Virus (VZV)**—the same virus responsible for **chickenpox**. After a person recovers from chickenpox, the virus lies dormant in the **nervous system** and can reactivate years later, particularly in older adults or individuals with weakened immune systems. Shingles presents with **painful skin rashes**, typically limited to one side of the body, and can sometimes lead to serious complications such as **postherpetic neuralgia (PHN)**. **So, statement I is not correct and Statement II is correct.**
● It is **not** classified as Neglected Tropical Disease (**NTD**). **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key facts about the Shingles:**
● After recovery from chickenpox, VZV becomes latent in the dorsal root ganglia, which are clusters of nerve cells near the spinal cord. The virus can lie dormant for decades before reactivating, typically due to a weakened immune system, stress, or age. When reactivated, it travels along the nerves to the skin, causing localized, painful rashes.
● Shingles usually present as **painful rashes and itching**, often with blistering lesions. The rash usually appears on one side of the body or face, following the path of a nerve, and is often described as burning, tingling, or itching.
● Age and immunosuppression are the two major risk factors for the reactivation of the Varicella-Zoster Virus. Individuals with HIV, cancer, or those undergoing chemotherapy or organ transplants are at especially high risk. As a preventive measure, the Shingrix vaccine is recommended for people over 50 years to reduce the risk and severity of shingles.
**Relevance:** In 2024 and 2025, India witnessed a significant surge in public health initiatives and research concerning shingles (herpes zoster).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
The principles of jus sanguinis (right of blood) and
jus soli (right of the soil) are primarily associated
with which one of the following domains?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The terms **'jus sanguinis'** and **'jus soli'** are used in the context of **citizenship and nationality laws**. These Latin terms describe the two primary principles by which countries determine a person’s citizenship at birth.
● **'Jus sanguinis'**, meaning ‘right of blood’, grants citizenship based on the nationality or ethnicity of one or both parents, regardless of the place of birth. This principle is commonly followed by countries like Germany and Japan.
● On the other hand, **'jus soli'**, meaning ‘right of the soil’, confers citizenship to anyone born on the country’s territory, regardless of their parents' nationality. This approach is typical in countries like the United States of America  and Canada. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the President of the USA signed a host of executive orders, including a move to end birthright citizenship.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - It is named after a Jaina tirthankara.
II. 	 Annual Sendra festival is celebrated here.
III. 	 Among Santhals, it is revered as Marang Buru i.e., ‘Great Mountain’.
Which hill is this?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Parasnath Hill holds immense religious significance as it is considered highly sacred by the** **Jain and Santhal (Adivasi) communities.** For Jains, the hill is known as **Parasnath, named after** **Parshvanatha, the 23rd tirthankara**, and is believed to be the site where 20 of the 24 tirthankaras attained nirvana. For the Santals, the hill is a vital spiritual and cultural landmark and is referred to as Marang Buru, meaning “Great Mountain.” The **Sendra festival is a significant Santhal tradition** **celebrated annually on the Parasnath hill.** The Sendra festival is an important and symbolic ritual observed annually by the Santal community. It involves a traditional tribal hunt and is deeply rooted in Santal customs and masculinity rituals.
● **Among the Santhals, one of India’s largest Adivasi communities, Parasnath Hill is revered** **as Marang Buru, which translates to “Great Mountain.**” It is not just a sacred place but the spiritual and judicial center of the Santal worldview. The hill houses the Jug Jaher Than, a sacred grove and serves as the venue for important rituals and decisions made by the Lo Bir Baisi, a tribal council that oversees disputes that cannot be resolved at the village level**.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Jharkhand High Court directed the State Government to enforce a pre-existing ban on the consumption and sale of meat, alcohol, and other intoxicants on a Parasnath hill.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV), consider the following statements:
I. - It has a three-stage cryogenic-based propellant engine.
II. 	 It is primarily designed for launching satellites into Sun-synchronous polar orbits.
III. 	 It can accommodate multiple payload adaptors for deploying several satellites in a single mission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) is a four-stage launch vehicle developed by the** **Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO).** It uses a combination of solid and liquid propellants, arranged in alternating stages. Specifically, the first and third stages use solid propellant, while the second and fourth stages use liquid propellant. Unlike the GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle), the **PSLV does not use a cryogenic engine at any stage. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The PSLV is primarily designed for launching satellites into Sun-synchronous polar orbits** **and low Earth orbits,** which are commonly used for remote sensing, Earth observation, and scientific missions. While the PSLV has demonstrated the capability to launch satellites into sub-Geosynchronous Transfer Orbits (GTOs) on rare occasions, it is not specialized or exclusively designed for Geosynchronous orbit missions. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The PSLV is capable of placing multiple payloads into orbit, thus multi-payload adaptors** **are used in the payload fairing.** The payload performance of the vehicle and mission flexibility is evident from the challenging missions where multi-orbit and multi-satellite missions are accomplished**.** **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** ISRO's PSLV-C61 mission, intended to deploy the EOS-09 earth observation satellite, failed after normal performance until the second stage.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Which of the following Indian States were created through an amendment to the Constitution of India?
I. - Sikkim
II. 	 Goa
III. 	 Telangana
IV. 	 Arunachal Pradesh
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
For inclusion of any new territory to the Indian Union a Constitutional Amendment as per Article 368 is required.
● **Sikkim**:  The **Constitution (36th Amendment) Act, 1975**, made Sikkim the 22nd State of India. This act was enacted after Sikkim's unanimous resolution to become a part of India and abolish the institution of the Chogyal. The amendment included provisions for Sikkim's representation in the Parliament. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Goa:** In 1962 the Indian Parliament passed the **12th Amendment Act, 1962** to the Constitution of India, under which the Territory of Goa, Daman & Diu was included in the Schedule I of the Constitution of India (Constitution) and was declared as an Union Territory.
○ Under the Constitution **(Fifty Sixth Amendment) Act, 1987** Goa was included in the Schedule I of the Constitution of India, as the 25th (twenty fifth) State of India. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● **Telangana:** The Andhra Pradesh Reorganisation Act (2014) bifurcated Andhra Pradesh into two separate states, namely, the Andhra Pradesh (residuary) and the Telangana. On 2nd June, 2014 the northwestern part of Andhra Pradesh was separated and 29th state Telangana was created. **So, point** **III is not correct.**
● **Arunachal Pradesh:** It officially obtained the title of State in 1987. It became the 24th State of India
under the State of Arunachal Pradesh Act, enacted through the **55th Constitutional Amendment,**
**1986**. **So, point IV is correct.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** May 16 is annually commemorated as Sikkim Day, marking the former kingdom’s integration with
India in 1975.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Demography, Population, Census (Literacy, Sex Ratio, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following States/Union Territory in ascending order of the Total Fertility Rate:
I. - Delhi
II. 	 Tamil Nadu
III. 	 Assam
IV. 	 Karnataka
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Total Fertility Rate (TFR)** is the average number of children per woman. It has remained constant at
2.0 in 2021, the same as the year 2020, according to the Sample Registration System (SRS) report for 2021 released by the Registrar General of India (RGI).
● **Bihar has reported the highest TFR at 3.0**, while Delhi and West Bengal reported the lowest TFR of 1.4.
● The report said there had been a **gradual decline in the share of population in the age group of** **0-14 from 41.2% in 1971 to 24.8% in 2021**. The “proportion of the economically active population between 15-59 years has increased from 53.4% to 66.2% during the same period,” the report said.
● The elderly population has gone up from 5.3% to 5.9% for 65+ age group and 6% to 9% for 60+ age group during the same period.
○ **Kerala recorded the highest percentage of population in the age group of 60** and above with 14.4% of the total population falling under this category. ○ **Tamil Nadu (12.9%) and Himachal Pradesh (12.3%)** are the other two States with the **highest percentage of elderly population**, the report said. ○ On the other hand, Bihar (6.9%), Assam (7%) and Delhi (7.1%) have the lowest percentage of the population in the age group of 60 and above. ○ The mean age at effective marriage for females has increased from 19.3 years in 1990 to 22.5 years in 2021.
● As the Census is usually counted every 10 years, SRS is the largest demographic survey in the country mandated to provide annual estimates of fertility and mortality indicators at the State and national level. The survey was conducted in 8,842 sample units across all States, covering about 84 lakh sample population.
● It is noteworthy that the **replacement level TFR, viz. 2.1, has been attained at the national** **level**, along with **Delhi 1.4**, West Bengal 1.4, **Tamil Nadu 1.5**, Andhra Pradesh 1.5, Jammu and Kashmir 1.5, Kerala 1.5, Maharashtra 1.5, Punjab 1.5, Himachal Pradesh 1.6, Telangana 1.6, **Karnataka**
**1.6**, Odisha 1.8, Uttarakhand 1.8, Gujarat 2.0, Haryana 2.0 and **Assam 2.1**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Total Fertility Rate (TFR) has remained constant at 2.0 in 2021, the same as the year 2020, according to the Sample Registration System (SRS) report for 2021 released by the Registrar General of India (RGI).

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: ‘One nation, One Heartbeat’, is an initiative of:

options.a: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In a heartfelt tribute to the undying spirit of patriotism and to honour the bravehearts of the nation, the **Ministry of Culture** has launched a powerful and binding initiative **“Ek Desh Ek Dhadkan” (One** **Nation, One Heartbeat).**
● Designed to unify the nation in a shared rhythm of national pride, the campaign has already begun to resonate strongly with citizens across India. The slogan **#EkDeshEkDhadkan** **(#OneNationOneHeartbeat)** is being widely adopted across digital platforms, **amplifying the spirit** **of unity, patriotic fervour and reverence for the Tiranga**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the PM E-DRIVE Scheme:
I. - It aims to speed up the transition to electric vehicles by offering upfront incentives for EV purchases.
II. 	 It seeks to boost domestic manufacturing and strengthen the EV supply chain through the Phased Manufacturing Program (PMP).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **PM E-DRIVE initiative promotes mass mobility through the support of public** **transportation systems**. The key objective is to **speed up the transition to electric vehicles by** **offering upfront incentives for EV purchases** and encouraging the development of charging infrastructure. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The scheme seeks to reduce transportation-related environmental impacts and improve air quality while also promoting an **efficient and competitive EV manufacturing sector** in line with the **Aatmanirbhar Bharat initiative**. This is to be accomplished through a **Phased Manufacturing** **Program (PMP)** designed to boost domestic manufacturing and strengthen the EV supply chain. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The scheme aims to promote the adoption of electric trucks to reduce CO2 emissions and establish etrucks as a key logistics solution in the future. A fund of Rs. 500 crore has been set aside to incentivize the use of e-trucks. **Only those holding a scrapping certificate from MoRTH-approved vehicle** **scrapping centers (RVSF) are eligible** for the incentives. Monitoring systems will verify scrapping certificates. The relevant details for e-trucks including number of vehicles to be supported, maximum subsidy, performance criteria, etc. will be notified separately based on consultation with relevant stakeholders. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Union Minister for Heavy Industries chaired an inter-ministerial coordination meeting to review and accelerate the implementation of EV charging infrastructure under the PM E-Drive scheme.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Eritrea
II. 	 Djibouti
III. 	 Kenya
IV. 	 Uganda
V. 	 Sudan
How many of the above countries share their land borders with Ethiopia?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ethiopia is a landlocked** country on the Horn of Africa. **The country lies completely within the** **tropical latitudes and is relatively compact**, with similar north-south and east-west dimensions. The capital is Addis Ababa (“New Flower”), located almost at the centre of the country. Ethiopia is the largest and most populated country in the Horn of Africa. With the 1993 secession of Eritrea, its former province along the Red Sea, Ethiopia became landlocked.
● **Ethiopia is bounded by Eritrea to the north, Djibouti to the northeast, Somalia to the east,** **Kenya to the south, and South Sudan and Sudan to the west. So, points I, II, III and V are** **correct.**
● **Uganda does not share its land borders with Ethiopia. So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only four of the above countries share their land borders with Ethiopia.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A high-level official delegation from Ethiopia comprising Governors, Vice-Governors and Ministers, recently visited India.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: The “Tankai method”, recently seen in the news, is best associated with which one of the following?

options.a: An ancient irrigation technique used in South India
options.b: A traditional form of textile weaving in Gujarat
options.c: A 2,000-year-old stitched shipbuilding technique using coir
options.d: A traditional salt harvesting method practiced along the Rann of Kutch

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under the “STITCHED SHIP” project, India aims to reconnect with its glorious maritime past. The revival of stitched shipbuilding stands as a symbol of national pride and is intended to serve as a bridge to the future, intertwining history with modern efforts to preserve and promote this rich heritage.
● The history of the **stitched shipbuilding method** in India, known as the “**Tankai method”**, dates back over 2,000 years. This unique technique involves **stitching wooden planks** **together using** **cords instead of nails** (Iron was consciously avoided during the construction of these ships).
● Unlike other shipbuilding methods, stitched ships possessed hulls with enhanced flexibility, **allowing** **them to navigate the perilous waters of the Indian subcontinent with ease**.
● The durability of these vessels made them ideal for long voyages, facilitating trade routes that connected ancient India with regions as far as **Southeast Asia**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The most reliable surviving evidence of Indian origin with respect to shipbuilding in ancient India is an **11th-century** Sanskrit text titled ‘**Yuktikalpataru**’. The authorship of this text is generally credited to **King Bhoja of Malwa**.
● Reputed Indian historian and nationalist, **Padma Bhushan Radha Kumud** **Mookerjee**, in his book titled **‘Indian Shipping: A History of the Sea-Borne Trade** **and Maritime Activity of the Indians from the Earliest Times’**, provides an extensive commentary on the aforementioned text.
● According to the **Yuktikalpataru**, the use of iron nails was prohibited in sea-going vessels due to the possible risk of exposing the vessel to magnetic rocks in the ocean. However, another plausible explanation for this avoidance may be attributed to the susceptibility of iron to rust.
● Therefore, in view of the poor material properties of iron nails, shipbuilders stuck to the technique of fastening or “stitching” the wooden planks together with coil yarn,
which was subsequently secured (or, in contemporary terms, made waterproof) by dipping it into fish oil. **Bhoja of the Paramara Dynasty:**
● His reign from **1010 CE to 1055 CE** marked the zenith of Paramara’s power and cultural achievements.
● He founded the city of Bhojpur, constructed the **Bhojeshwar Temple** and built three dams in the area. He also founded the Bhoj Shala, a centre for Sanskrit studies in Dhar.
**Relevance:** Recently, the Ministry of Culture and the Indian Navy have collaborated to revive and preserve the 2,000-year-old 'Tankai' technique, also known as the "stitched shipbuilding method".

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Electric Vehicles (EVs) can lead to microplastic pollution.
**Statement II:** EVs experience higher wear and tear in their tyres compared to conventional vehicles.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Electric Vehicles (EVs) play an important role in the fight against climate change by eliminating Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions. However, while contributing to solving one problem, they may be adding to another.
● The study in a journal of the Royal Society of Chemistry has shown that EVs, on account of their greater weight, experience **higher wear and tear in their tyres compared to conventional vehicles** and **release substantially larger numbers of small plastic particles in the atmosphere**. **So,** **statements I and II are correct.**
● The disintegration of automobile tyres results in the release of **small rubber particles** that are air pollutants.
○ The normal wear and tear of tyres produces particles of broadly two sizes – one, about **1-10** **micrometres**; the other, **more than 100 micrometres**.
● The study by researchers from the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR), IIT Bombay and Columbia University in the US has, for the first time, established the relationship between the weight and speed of a vehicle to the size of the plastic particles released from tyres as a result of wear and tear.
● The particles of different sizes are produced by different physical processes of degradation of tyres: ○ **Primary fragmentation** (e.g. braking, potholes) → **smaller particles** ○ **Sequential fragmentation** (gradual wear) → **larger particles**
● EVs are **15–20% heavier** than petrol vehicles due to **heavy batteries (300–900 kg)** and reinforced frames. Their ability to accelerate rapidly increases tyre stress, leading to more primary fragmentation and release of smaller plastic particles into the air. The study highlights the **lesser-known negative** **impacts** of EVs on **air pollution**, despite their climate benefits. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Road transport contributes nearly **10% of global GHG emissions**, mostly from fuel
burning.
● According to an International Energy Agency report, **EVs form 2% of global vehicle**
**stock**, but made up **20% of new car sales last year.**
● In **India**, 2.5% of cars sold in 2024 were EVs; the **target is 30% by 2030**.
● In **China**, EVs accounted for **almost half of all car sales** last year.
**Relevance:** The recent study has shown that EVs release more plastic particles due to greater tyre wear from
their heavier weight compared to conventional vehicles.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText: ****India’s recent steps to include climate hazards in its National Adaptation Plan (NAP) are in line with which one of the following international agreements?

options.c: Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As climate-induced hazards multiply manifold, India is preparing a National Adaptation Plan (NAP), a **first-of-its-kind national policy response aimed at reducing the vulnerability of communities** across the country. The plan primarily focuses on **building adaptive capacities and resilience at a** **national scale**.
● India has officially launched its NAP process to enhance climate resilience. The 2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference **(COP30)**, to be held in **Brazil from 10 to 21 November 2025**, is where **India’s NAP will be submitted** to the **UNFCCC secretariat**.
● India already has in place its **National Action Plan on Climate Change** and is now preparing its **National Adaptation Plan in compliance with the Paris Agreement.** The NAP also aims to facilitate the integration of climate change adaptation with relevant upcoming and existing policies, programmes, development planning processes and strategies.
● The NAP process was **established during COP16, 2024** to help countries identify medium and longterm adaptation needs and to develop and implement strategies and programmes to address those needs.
● National Adaptation Plans are crucial for **developing countries** to identify their **resilience-building** **needs** and to **access necessary funding** for adaptation actions. According to the **UNFCCC**, **142** **developing countries** that are Parties to it are undertaking measures to formulate and implement NAPs. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Environment, Forest and Climate Change recently said that India will submit its first National Adaptation Plan (NAP) to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) in the coming months.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Coronal Mass Ejections (CMEs):
I. - They primarily release energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation.
II. 	 They lead to the expulsion of large amounts of plasma and magnetic fields into space.
III. 	 They can cause geomagnetic storms upon interacting with Earth's magnetosphere.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Flares and CMEs are explosive events in the Sun. They are due to magnetic reconnection during which magnetic field lines rearrange.
● During a flare, energy is released primarily as **electromagnetic radiation** from the heated plasma. Compared to flares, the **CMEs are massive eruptions of plasma and magnetic field** weighing about trillion kilograms that race outwards from the Sun through the **interplanetary space at speeds** **up to 3,000 kilometres per second**. The association between flares and CMEs is ambiguous till date. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Following a flare, high-speed solar particles, including protons, may arrive within one to two hours, posing risks to astronauts, satellites and electronic systems. Additionally, such solar storms can heat the upper atmosphere, causing it to expand, which increases drag on low-orbit satellites, potentially affecting their orbits.
● CME eruptions consist of **electrons, protons and embedded magnetic fields** traveling at high speeds. While CMEs do involve electromagnetic radiation, their defining characteristic is the **expulsion** **of large amounts of plasma and magnetic fields into space. So, statement II is correct.**
● CMEs can **cause geomagnetic storms** when they interact with **Earth s magnetosphere**. These interactions disrupt Earth's magnetic field, leading to **geomagnetic storms** that can affect a range of ground- and space-based technologies and satellites. **So,** **statement III is correct**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, astronomers witnessed a spectacular solar event — a massive ‘bird wing’-shaped eruption stretching nearly 6,00,000 miles from the Sun’s surface.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Which one of the following legal doctrines assumes that a law enacted by the legislature is valid and constitutional until it is declared otherwise by a court?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Doctrine of Presumption of Constitutionality** is a foundational principle of Indian constitutional law that upholds the **authority of the legislature and respects the democratic** **process.** According to this doctrine, any law enacted by the **Parliament or State Legislature is** **presumed to be constitutional** unless it is clearly proven to be in violation of the Constitution.
● The burden of proof lies on the person or entity challenging the law, not on the State. This principle reinforces the idea that the legislature acts within its constitutional limits and promotes judicial restraint in invalidating laws.
● The doctrine is deeply rooted in the **separation of powers,** ensuring that the judiciary does not overstep its role in reviewing legislative action. The **Supreme Court of India** has repeatedly affirmed this doctrine, holding that courts must interpret laws in a way that upholds their constitutionality wherever possible and only strike them down when there is a clear and unmistakable infringement of constitutional provisions. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **Doctrine of Eclipse** dictates that pre-constitutional laws become dormant but
not void if they clash with Fundamental Rights enshrined in the Constitution.
● The **Doctrine of Severability** is a legal principle that allows a contract or law to
remain enforceable even if some parts are found to be invalid or unconstitutional. If a
provision is deemed invalid, it can be "severed" or removed, leaving the remaining valid
portions in effect.
● The **Doctrine of Basic Structure** asserts that certain fundamental features of the
Constitution cannot be altered or destroyed by Parliament through constitutional
amendments. This doctrine, established in the landmark **Kesavananda Bharati case**
**(1973).**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court recently in the Waqf Amendment Act, 2025 observed that a parliamentary
statute enjoys a presumption of constitutionality.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding inter crystals:
I. - They display electronic properties and exhibit non-repeating atomic arrangements.
II. 	 Unlike superconductors, inter crystals conduct electricity with consistently high resistance at all temperatures.
III. 	 They can be used in advanced applications like superconductivity, quantum computing and sustainable materials.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Inter Crystals** represent a breakthrough class of materials recently discovered by researchers in Canada. These materials possess unique and tunable electronic properties, distinguishing them from both **conventional crystals and quasicrystals.**
● One of the defining features of inter crystals is their **sensitivity to minor geometrical adjustments.** It was discovered that simply twisting two atom-thick graphene layers slightly over a boron nitride base alters the movement of electrons due to the formation of **moiré patterns.** This process—referred to as **"twistronics"**—enables a high degree of tunability in **electronic properties** such as conductivity, magnetism and energy band structures. This implies that researchers can fine-tune the properties of inter crystals by adjusting twist angles and stacking orders, offering precise control over material behavior without altering their chemistry.
● They combine characteristics of **both regular crystals and quasicrystals.** While they share certain symmetries found in traditional crystals, they do not have the repeating atomic patterns characteristic of them. Instead**, like quasicrystals, the inter crystals possess non-repeating arrangements.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● Inter Crystals have shown potential for **superconductivity, where electrical resistance drops to** **zero.** The unique electron interactions enabled by moiré patterns and structural tuning allow inter crystals to enter exotic electronic phases, including superconducting states. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● The researchers found that intercrystals could host behaviors such as **superconductivity,** in which materials conduct electricity with zero resistance and **magnetism,** opening avenues in **quantum** **computing and sensor development.** Additionally, since inter crystals can be fabricated using abundant and non-toxic elements like carbon, boron and nitrogen, they offer a **scalable and eco-** **friendly alternative to rare-earth-based electronics. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, researchers have discovered a new class of materials—called inter crystals.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: The Hoyle–Narlikar theory  is related to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Hoyle–Narlikar theory of gravity**, developed in 1964 by eminent Indian astrophysicist **Jayant** **Narlikar** and British astronomer **Fred Hoyle**, is a unique **cosmological model based on conformal** **gravity.** It sought to address some limitations of Einstein’s General Theory of Relativity by incorporating **Mach’s Principle** more rigorously.
● **Mach’s Principle**, attributed to physicist **Ernst Mach**, proposes that the **inertia of an object** is not an intrinsic property but rather **arises from its gravitational interaction with all the matter in** **the universe**. Einstein found this idea compelling, but could not successfully incorporate it into his theory of General Relativity. Hoyle and Narlikar, however, took Mach’s Principle seriously and made it central to their theory. They posited that **mass is relational**, meaning an object cannot have mass in an otherwise empty universe. In their view, the gravitational influence of distant galaxies and stars contributes to the mass and inertia of local bodies, giving a **universal connectedness to matter**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Astrophysicist Jayant Narlikar passed away.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.microTopic: Allied Sectors (Animal Husbandry, Horticulture, Sericulture, Apiculture, Aquaculture)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the State of the World’s Animal Health Report 2025 released by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH):
I. - Nearly half of the diseases notified between 2005 and 2023 were zoonotic.
II. 	 International trade has been cited as one of the key drivers in the spread and prevalence of animal diseases.
III. 	 Human-to-animal transmission is the primary route for most animal diseases.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **State of the World’s Animal Health Report 2025**, released by the **World Organisation for** **Animal Health (WOAH),** provides a comprehensive overview of the global status of infectious animal diseases. It highlights the dynamic and evolving threats posed by diseases that impact animals, agriculture, food systems, public health and biodiversity.
● According to the WOAH report, nearly **47 percent** of the diseases notified between 2005 and 2023 were **zoonotic,** meaning they had the potential to infect humans. These include well-known diseases like **avian influenza, Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) and African Swine Fever (ASF)**. Zoonotic diseases are especially concerning because they can cross species barriers, posing risks to public health, food safety and even global security. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The report explicitly identifies **climate change** and **international trade** as two of the most influential factors reshaping the global animal disease landscape. For instance, **rising temperatures** and **shifting** **weather patterns** are changing the habitats of disease vectors like midges and mosquitoes, leading to the spread of diseases such as **Bluetongue virus** into previously unaffected regions. Additionally, the **expansion of global trade and livestock movement** has facilitated the transboundary transmission of diseases such as **ASF**, which in 2024 jumped more than **1,800 kilometers** to reach Sri Lanka. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Human-to-animal transmission** is **not the primary route** for most animal diseases. Instead, many diseases spread through **animal-to-animal contact**, environmental contamination or **vector-borne** **transmission** via insects like ticks and flies. For instance, **ASF spreads through wild boars and** **contaminated feed**, not human contact. Similarly, **Bluetongue virus** is transmitted by **biting midges** and **avian influenza** often spreads through migratory birds. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the inaugural State of the World’s Animal Health report, infectious animal diseases are spreading to previously unaffected regions and species, with nearly half (47 per cent) capable of zoonotic transmission or spreading between animals.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: Why is it challenging to observe liquid carbon under standard laboratory conditions?

options.a: It reacts vigorously with air and water.
options.b: It remains stable only at cryogenic (extremely low) temperatures.
options.c: It transitions directly from solid to gas under normal atmospheric pressure.
options.d: It quickly solidifies into graphite or diamond before forming a stable liquid phase.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Liquid carbon**, a state of carbon found under **extreme conditions** such as in planetary interiors or during nuclear fusion processes, has long eluded detailed scientific observation under normal laboratory settings. This is primarily due to its extreme physical requirements - **carbon does not melt under** **ordinary pressure but sublimates directly into gas** and only under **very high pressures and** **temperatures** (around 4500°C) does it enter a liquid phase. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Structure of liquid carbon has been measured for the first time.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following industries:
I. - Electricity
II. 	 Fertilisers
III. 	 Automobiles
IV. 	 Cement
V. 	 Textiles
How many of the above are **not** amongst the eight core industries under the Index of Industrial Production (IIP)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Index of Industrial Production (IIP)** tracks the short-term changes in the volume of production across major sectors of the Indian economy. The **IIP is published by the Central Statistics** **Office(CSO)**, which is part of the **Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation** **(MoSPI)**. The **current base year** for the index is **2011–12**.
○ This base year was adopted to reflect **modern industrial structure and production** **patterns**, following periodic revisions from earlier base years such as **1937, 1946, 1951, 1956**, and so on.
● Recently, the combined Index of Eight Core Industries (ICI) increased by 0.5 per cent (provisional) in April 2025 as compared to the Index in April 2024. The production of Cement, Coal, Steel, Electricity and Natural Gas recorded positive growth in April 2025.
● Within this, the **Eight Core Industries** are particularly significant, as they have a combined weight of
**40.27% in the overall IIP.**
● These **Eight Core Industries,** listed in decreasing order of weight, are: ○ Refinery Products - 28.04% ○ **Electricity - 19.85%** ○ Steel - 17.92% ○ Coal - 10.33% ○ Crude Oil - 8.98% ○ Natural Gas - 6.88% ○ **Cement - 5.37%** ○ **Fertilisers - 2.63.**
● **Automobiles** and **Textiles** are **not** among the Eight Core Industries. **So, only two of the above are** **not** **among the eight core industries under the Index of Industrial** **Production (IIP).** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Index of Industrial Production (IIP) slowed to an eight-month low of 2.7% in April, at the start of fiscal 2026.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Communities sometimes mentioned in the news
Countries associated
I. - Alawites Yemen
II. - Uighurs China
III. - Houthis Iran
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Alawites (Syria):** Alawite community is a **Shia sect of Islam.** The Alawites, a minority, had dominated government posts under Assad. The group that has come to power after his fall, Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS), is an al-Qaeda offshoot. Alawites and other Syrian minorities, such as **Christians and the** **Druze,** fear they will face persecution under this hardline Sunni regime, although interim President Ahmed al-Sharaa has pledged to carry everyone along. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Uighurs (China):** The **Uighurs live in Xinjiang,** the largest and most western of **China’s** **administrative regions,** which is surrounded by Mongolia, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, Pakistan and India. The United States and other countries made repeated offers to Thailand to resettle more than three dozen Uighurs men before they were deported back to China, where rights groups fear they may face torture and other abuse. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Houthis (Yemen):** The Houthis are a large clan belonging to the **Zaidi Shia sect,** with roots in **Yemen’s northwestern Saada province.** They make up around 35 per cent of Yemen’s population.
